facts.text: Rites of Spring, formed in Washington D.C. in 1984, are widely considered the first emo band, blending hardcore punk with deeply personal, emotional lyrics.
The term 'emo' was originally short for 'emocore' (emotional hardcore) and was coined in the mid-1980s D.C. punk scene.
My Chemical Romance's 'The Black Parade' (2006) is a rock opera concept album about a dying cancer patient who reflects on his life.
Fugazi, fronted by Ian MacKaye, refused to sell merchandise, kept ticket prices to $5, and never signed to a major label throughout their career.
The Fender Jazzmaster became the quintessential emo guitar due to its warm, jangly tone and affordable vintage pricing in the 1990s.
Fall Out Boy's 'From Under the Cork Tree' debuted at No. 9 on the Billboard 200 and eventually went double platinum.
Saetia, a seminal screamo band from New York City, existed for only three years (1997–2000) but profoundly influenced the genre.
Fueled By Ramen, founded in 1996 by Vinnie Fiorello of Less Than Jake and John Janick, became the most commercially successful emo label of the 2000s.
The Vans Warped Tour ran annually from 1995 to 2019, serving as a launching pad for countless emo and pop-punk bands.
American Football's self-titled debut LP was largely ignored on release in 1999 but became one of the most influential emo albums ever after the band broke up.
Dashboard Confessional was one of the first acts to build a massive following through acoustic emo, with frontman Chris Carrabba performing solo with just a guitar.
Gerard Way, frontman of My Chemical Romance, created the Eisner Award-winning comic book series 'The Umbrella Academy,' later adapted into a Netflix show.
Sunny Day Real Estate's 'Diary' (1994) is often cited as the album that defined second-wave emo, blending alternative rock with raw emotional intensity.
Cap'n Jazz, featuring members who would later form The Promise Ring, Owen, and American Football, only released one full-length album before disbanding in 1995.
Twinkly guitar tapping—a hallmark of Midwest emo—was popularized by bands like American Football and Cap'n Jazz using intricate fingerpicking and tapping techniques.
Brand New's 'Deja Entendu' (2003) is considered one of the greatest emo albums ever made, featuring the iconic artwork of an astronaut in a swimming pool.
Hot Topic stores became synonymous with emo culture in the 2000s, selling band tees, studded belts, and skinny jeans to suburban teens nationwide.
Thursday's 'Full Collapse' (2001) was one of the first post-hardcore albums to cross over to mainstream audiences, thanks in part to MTV2 airplay.
The Promise Ring evolved from the ashes of Cap'n Jazz, with Davey von Bohlen shifting from chaotic Midwest emo to a more polished, pop-influenced sound.
Dischord Records, founded by Ian MacKaye and Jeff Nelson in 1980, released music by Rites of Spring, Embrace, and Fugazi, making it the birthplace label of emo.
Pete Wentz of Fall Out Boy co-founded Decaydance Records (later DCD2), signing Panic! At The Disco and Cobra Starship.
Orchid, a screamo band from Amherst, Massachusetts, played shows that rarely lasted more than 15 minutes due to their extremely short, explosive songs.
Jimmy Eat World's 'Bleed American' was originally self-funded after the band was dropped by Capitol Records, and it became their breakthrough album.
The side-swept fringe hairstyle became the most recognizable visual element of emo culture in the mid-2000s.
At the Drive-In's 'Relationship of Command' (2000) was produced by Ross Robinson and Iggy Pop, blending post-hardcore with avant-garde intensity.
Glassjaw's 'Worship and Tribute' (2002) pushed post-hardcore into heavier, more experimental territory with Daryl Palumbo's dynamic vocal range.
Myspace was the dominant platform for emo bands from roughly 2004 to 2008, allowing unsigned artists to build massive followings through profile plays.
Mineral, from Austin, Texas, released only two albums before breaking up in 1998 but became hugely influential in the emo revival of the 2010s.
Underoath successfully blended metalcore with post-hardcore and emo, becoming one of the heaviest bands to achieve mainstream crossover success.
The Gibson ES-335 semi-hollow body guitar has been favored by many emo and post-hardcore guitarists for its rich, resonant clean tones.
Taking Back Sunday and Brand New had a famous rivalry, with both bands writing songs attacking each other's members.
Jawbreaker's '24 Hour Revenge Therapy' (1994) bridged punk and emo with Blake Schwarzenbach's literary lyrics and raw vocal delivery.
Panic! At The Disco's debut album 'A Fever You Can't Sweat Out' was released when most band members were still teenagers.
Texas Is the Reason existed for only two years (1994–1996) but their sole album 'Do You Know Who You Are?' became an emo touchstone.
Circle Takes the Square combined screamo with progressive and psychedelic elements, creating some of the most ambitious music in the genre.
Victory Records, founded by Tony Brummel in 1989, was home to Thursday, Hawthorne Heights, and Silverstein during the 2000s emo boom.
The Klon Centaur overdrive pedal, known for its transparent gain, became a coveted piece of gear among emo guitarists seeking warm, dynamic distortion.
Saosin's original vocalist Anthony Green (later of Circa Survive) was replaced by Cove Reber, creating two distinct eras of the band's sound.
LiveJournal and Xanga were key platforms for emo kids to share poetry, song lyrics, and emotional confessions in the early 2000s.
Embrace, Ian MacKaye's band after Minor Threat, is considered one of the founding emo bands despite only releasing one self-titled album in 1987.
Modern Baseball emerged from a college dorm room at Drexel University, becoming one of the most beloved bands of the 2010s emo revival.
Pierce the Veil blended post-hardcore with Latin-influenced rhythms, reflecting frontman Vic Fuentes' Mexican-American heritage.
The Bamboozle Festival in New Jersey (2003–2012) was one of the premier emo and pop-punk festivals on the East Coast.
Envy, from Tokyo, Japan, brought a cinematic, post-rock influenced approach to screamo that earned them international acclaim.
My Chemical Romance formed in Newark, New Jersey, just days after the September 11 attacks, which profoundly influenced their early music.
Boss DD-series delay pedals are a staple of emo guitar rigs, used to create the genre's characteristic atmospheric, layered guitar sounds.
Julien Baker's debut album 'Sprained Ankle' was recorded in a single day and launched her as a leading voice in modern emo.
The Used's self-titled debut (2002) went platinum, with vocalist Bert McCracken's raw screams and confessional lyrics resonating with a generation.
pg.99 (pageninetynine) from Sterling, Virginia, combined screamo with noise rock and post-punk, influencing the entire screamo underground.
Chiodos featured classical piano alongside post-hardcore instrumentation, with keyboardist Bradley Bell adding a unique orchestral dimension.
Vagrant Records, home to Dashboard Confessional, The Get Up Kids, and Saves the Day, was a cornerstone label of early 2000s emo.
Dance Gavin Dance pioneered the 'swancore' sound—a blend of post-hardcore, funk, R&B, and progressive rock centered around guitarist Will Swan.
The Get Up Kids' 'Something to Write Home About' (1999) helped define the Midwest emo sound and was one of Vagrant Records' biggest early releases.
Emo's mainstream peak is often pinpointed to 2006–2007, when My Chemical Romance, Fall Out Boy, and Panic! At The Disco dominated MTV and radio.
Skinny jeans became a defining fashion element of emo culture, with many fans and musicians wearing them regardless of gender.
Hawthorne Heights' 'Ohio Is for Lovers' became an emo anthem, with its chorus capturing the melodramatic romanticism central to the genre.
Phoebe Bridgers, though often classified as indie folk, cites emo as a major influence and has covered songs by emo bands.
Silverstein have released over ten studio albums since 2000, making them one of the most prolific and consistent post-hardcore bands.
City of Caterpillar, from Richmond, Virginia, blended screamo with post-rock atmospherics, foreshadowing the post-metal movement.
Open tunings, particularly FACGCE, became central to Midwest emo guitar work, allowing for complex, interlocking finger-tapped melodies.
A Day to Remember successfully merged pop-punk melodies with metalcore breakdowns, creating a crossover style sometimes called 'easycore.'
Deep Elm Records, based in New York, specialized in emo and released its 'Emo Diaries' compilation series documenting the genre's evolution.
Brand New's 'The Devil and God Are Raging Inside Me' (2006) marked a dramatic artistic shift, incorporating shoegaze and post-rock into their sound.
Jimmy Eat World formed in Mesa, Arizona, in 1993, making them one of the longest-running bands with roots in the emo genre.
The 'emo' label became controversial in the mid-2000s, with many bands rejecting it and media outlets using it to sensationalize teen culture.
Sleeping with Sirens' vocalist Kellin Quinn is known for his extremely high vocal range, often singing in a near-falsetto register.
Jade Tree Records released albums by The Promise Ring, Jets to Brazil, and Pedro the Lion, helping to shape the late-'90s emo landscape.
Mom Jeans, from Berkeley, California, represent the playful, self-deprecating side of the 2010s emo revival.
Capo usage at high frets became a common technique in emo, allowing guitarists to access bright, jangly chord voicings on standard guitars.
Taking Back Sunday's 'Tell All Your Friends' (2002) went gold and featured the dual vocal interplay between Adam Lazzara and John Nolan.
The Hotelier's 'Home, Like Noplace Is There' (2014) was a critical landmark of the emo revival, tackling mental health and loss with unflinching honesty.
Matt Pryor of The Get Up Kids later formed The New Amsterdams and pursued a solo career in alt-country, demonstrating emo's diverse musical offshoots.
Emo nights—DJ events playing classic emo and pop-punk hits—became a nationwide phenomenon in the late 2010s, filling clubs with nostalgic millennials.
Reverb-heavy guitar tones define much of the emo revival sound, with bands using pedals like the Electro-Harmonix Holy Grail and Boss RV-6.
Saves the Day's 'Through Being Cool' (1999) brought a faster, more poppy approach to emo that influenced the genre's commercial direction.
Prince Daddy & The Hyena blend emo with indie rock and garage punk, representing the genre's expanding sonic boundaries in the 2020s.
Underoath's 'They're Only Chasing Safety' (2004) sold over 500,000 copies, making it one of the best-selling post-hardcore albums of the decade.
Origami Angel's blend of emo, pop-punk, and math rock earned them a devoted following in the late 2010s emo revival scene.
The Fender Jaguar's short scale length and jangly pickups made it another favorite offset guitar among emo guitarists alongside the Jazzmaster.
Rise Records became a major post-hardcore and metalcore label in the late 2000s, signing Dance Gavin Dance, Of Mice & Men, and Crown the Empire.
Riot Fest, held annually in Chicago, has become one of the premier festivals for emo and punk reunion shows.
American Football reunited in 2014 after 15 years apart, releasing two more studio albums to critical acclaim.
Charmer, from Detroit, Michigan, craft melodic emo with intricate guitar work that draws from both Midwest emo and power pop traditions.
The 'Midwest emo' subgenre is characterized by complex, interlocking guitar parts, whispery vocals, and introspective lyrics about suburban ennui.
Sunny Day Real Estate broke up twice—first in 1995, then again in 2001—before briefly reuniting for a tour in 2009.
Eyeliner worn by male musicians became an iconic emo fashion statement, challenging mainstream gender norms in rock music.
Pete Wentz's bass lines in Fall Out Boy were heavily influenced by funk and new wave, giving the band a rhythmic backbone distinct from most emo acts.
Thursday's Geoff Rickly produced albums for bands like Thursday and My Chemical Romance's debut, helping shape the East Coast post-hardcore sound.
Circa Survive, formed by Anthony Green after leaving Saosin, developed a progressive, psychedelic take on post-hardcore.
Jawbreaker's signing to DGC Records in 1994 and subsequent album 'Dear You' was considered a controversial move by the punk community.
The 'emo trinity' of the 2000s refers to My Chemical Romance, Fall Out Boy, and Panic! At The Disco.
Weezer's 'Pinkerton' (1996), though not strictly emo, profoundly influenced the genre with its confessional lyrics and raw guitar sound.
Glassjaw frontman Daryl Palumbo also leads the electronic project Head Automatica, showcasing emo musicians' artistic range.
Funeral for a Friend, from Wales, brought British post-hardcore to international audiences with their 2003 debut 'Casually Dressed & Deep in Conversation.'
The Promise Ring's 'Nothing Feels Good' (1997) was named after a book of the same title that later became the definitive history of emo.
Many emo bands favored the humbucker-equipped Epiphone Les Paul as an affordable workhorse guitar for heavy rhythm tones.
Braid, from Champaign, Illinois, were contemporaries of American Football and helped define the intricate, mathy side of Midwest emo.
Dashboard Confessional's 'MTV Unplugged 2.0' was a watershed moment, proving that emo could fill arenas with just an acoustic guitar.
My Chemical Romance's 'I'm Not Okay (I Promise)' video was styled like a teen movie and became one of the most iconic emo music videos.
Paramore, though often grouped with pop-punk, incorporated significant emo and post-hardcore elements, especially on their earlier albums.
The emo revival (roughly 2011–present) brought a return to the genre's DIY roots, with bands self-releasing on Bandcamp and small labels.
Fall Out Boy went on hiatus from 2009 to 2013, during which Pete Wentz and Patrick Stump pursued solo projects.
Pianos and keyboards became more common in 2000s emo, with bands like Something Corporate and The Hush Sound featuring them prominently.
Brand New's Jesse Lacey was known for his literary, deeply introspective songwriting that drew from authors like Fyodor Dostoevsky.
Samiam, from Berkeley, California, were an early emo-adjacent band who bridged the gap between Gilman Street punk and emotional hardcore.
The split EP format was popular in the emo and screamo underground, allowing two bands to share vinyl releases and cross-pollinate fanbases.
Sleeping with Sirens' 'If You Were a Movie, This Would Be Your Soundtrack' EP blended acoustic emo with symphonic pop arrangements.
Emo's relationship with mental health has been complex—critics accused the genre of glorifying depression, while fans found solace and community in the music.
Fugazi's 'Repeater' (1990) is considered a foundational post-hardcore album, influencing virtually every band that followed in the genre.
Chris Conley of Saves the Day has cited The Smiths and The Cure as major influences, connecting emo to earlier melancholic rock traditions.
Panic! At The Disco's 'I Write Sins Not Tragedies' became one of the biggest emo-adjacent hits of 2006, reaching No. 7 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Studded belts, wristbands, and Converse Chuck Taylors were essential accessories in the emo fashion of the mid-2000s.
Foxing, from St. Louis, Missouri, blended emo with orchestral instrumentation and post-rock, expanding the genre's sonic palette.
The Appleseed Cast evolved from emo roots into expansive, atmospheric post-rock over their extensive discography.
Jimmy Eat World's 'The Middle' became a crossover pop hit in 2002, reaching No. 5 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Emo vinyl collecting became a major hobby, with original pressings of American Football LP1 selling for hundreds of dollars.
Silverstein's 'Discovering the Waterfront' (2005) showcased the Canadian band's ability to balance melodic hooks with intense breakdowns.
The phrase 'real emo only consists of...' became an internet copypasta debating what constitutes authentic emo music.
Piebald combined emo with funk and humor, standing out from the more serious tone of their contemporaries in the early 2000s.
My Chemical Romance broke up in 2013 but reunited in 2019 for a massive return tour that sold out arenas worldwide.
The Saddle Creek label from Omaha, Nebraska, released albums by Cursive and The Good Life, bands that straddled emo and indie rock.
Emo bands frequently used extremely long, poetic song titles—Fall Out Boy's 'Our Lawyer Made Us Change the Name of This Song So We Wouldn't Get Sued' being a prime example.
Pedro the Lion, led by David Bazan, brought a quieter, more contemplative approach to emo with folk and indie rock influences.
Chiodos' 'All's Well That Ends Well' (2005) was one of the most musically ambitious post-hardcore albums, featuring operatic vocal passages.
The emo subgenre 'twinkle daddy' emerged as a tongue-in-cheek label for emo revival bands using clean, twinkling guitar tones.
Ian MacKaye of Fugazi and Minor Threat is one of the most influential figures in punk and emo history, championing DIY ethics and straight edge.
Patrick Stump of Fall Out Boy is a multi-instrumentalist who plays guitar, drums, and piano in addition to his vocal duties.
Senses Fail's 'Let It Enfold You' (2004) blended post-hardcore intensity with pop-punk accessibility, going gold in the U.S.
The Get Up Kids are often credited with being the first emo band to achieve significant mainstream success in the late 1990s.
Andy Hull of Manchester Orchestra frequently collaborates with the emo and indie rock worlds, producing for bands like Kevin Devine.
My Chemical Romance's debut album 'I Brought You My Bullets, You Brought Me Your Love' was released on Eyeball Records before they signed to Reprise.
The trebly, scooped mid-tone guitar sound of early emo was achieved by bands using solid-state amps and single-coil pickups.
Touché Amoré blends screamo with melodic post-hardcore, with vocalist Jeremy Bolm's cathartic delivery earning widespread critical acclaim.
Fall Out Boy's 'Sugar, We're Goin Down' spent 24 weeks on the Billboard Hot 100, peaking at No. 8.
The cello, played by Matt Pryor in early recordings, was an uncommon but effective addition to some emo arrangements.
La Dispute combines spoken word poetry with post-hardcore instrumentation, creating a unique and emotionally intense listening experience.
Emo pop—the most commercially successful emo offshoot—emphasized catchy choruses and pop song structures over the genre's punk roots.
Motion City Soundtrack incorporated Moog synthesizers into their emo-pop sound, adding an electronic texture unusual for the genre.
Bright Eyes, led by Conor Oberst, was adjacent to the emo scene and shared bills and fans with many emo bands in the early 2000s.
Thrice evolved from straightforward post-hardcore into a boundary-pushing band exploring progressive rock, folk, and electronic elements.
The World Is a Beautiful Place & I Am No Longer Afraid to Die use collective, post-rock-influenced arrangements in their emo revival sound.
Reggie and the Full Effect was a side project of Get Up Kids bassist James Dewees, blending synth-pop with emo humor.
My Chemical Romance's 'Welcome to the Black Parade' hit No. 9 on the Billboard Hot 100 and became the band's signature anthem.
Screamo vocals—characterized by raw, throat-shredding screams—originated in the early '90s D.C. and San Diego hardcore scenes.
Converge's 'Jane Doe' (2001), while primarily metalcore, heavily influenced the heavier end of the emo and screamo spectrum.
Many emo guitarists favor chorus pedals like the Boss CE-2 to add shimmery width to clean guitar passages.
The 'emo kid' stereotype—black clothes, heavy eyeliner, emotional sensitivity—was both embraced and resisted by fans of the genre.
Cursive's 'The Ugly Organ' (2003) featured a cellist and deconstructed the emo formula with meta-lyrical themes about the music industry.
Fall Out Boy's Pete Wentz wrote most of the band's lyrics, while Patrick Stump composed the music and melodies.
Algebra One, an early emo band from Florida, contributed to the late-'80s emo scene that was largely centered in the D.C. area.
Sunny Day Real Estate's Jeremy Enigk released the acclaimed solo album 'Return of the Frog Queen' during the band's first hiatus.
The 'ugly crying' vocal delivery—wavering between singing and sobbing—became a distinctive stylistic hallmark of many emo vocalists.
Boygenius, the supergroup of Julien Baker, Phoebe Bridgers, and Lucy Dacus, has roots in the emo and indie traditions.
Taking Back Sunday's debut 'Tell All Your Friends' was largely written about the fallout of personal relationships within the Long Island music scene.
Emo bands frequently self-released 7-inch vinyl singles, EPs, and split releases, keeping the DIY punk ethos alive.
The Catherine Wheel and Hum, while shoegaze/alternative, influenced the heavy, effects-laden guitar tones many emo bands adopted.
Panic! At The Disco's debut album was written largely by guitarist Ryan Ross, who drew heavily from literary and theatrical influences.
Into It. Over It., the project of Evan Thomas Weiss, documented life across all 50 U.S. states through an emo lens on his album '52 Weeks.'
Emo's Midwest heartland—including cities like Champaign, Chicago, and Milwaukee—produced a disproportionate number of influential bands.
The 'three chords and the truth' punk ethos evolved in emo to favor complex time signatures and intricate guitar interplay.
Say Anything's 'Is a Real Boy' (2004) was a darkly comedic, theatrical take on emo that earned a cult following.
New Found Glory, though primarily pop-punk, shared significant crossover with the emo scene throughout the 2000s.
The kazoo solo in American Football's 'Never Meant' was an unexpected and endearing touch in one of emo's most iconic songs.
Sunny Day Real Estate reunited in 2009 to record a new album, marking their first new music in nearly a decade.
Dashboard Confessional's Chris Carrabba got his start in the punk band Further Seems Forever before going solo.
Small Brown Bike, from Marshall, Michigan, were part of the Midwest emo and post-hardcore scene centered around No Idea Records.
Emo has had significant scenes in unlikely places, including the Philippines, Indonesia, Japan, and Brazil.
The 'wave' system of emo history divides the genre into first wave (1980s D.C.), second wave (1990s indie), third wave (2000s mainstream), and fourth wave (2010s revival).
Finch's 'What It Is to Burn' (2002) was one of the first post-hardcore albums to receive significant commercial radio airplay.
Rites of Spring's Guy Picciotto went on to join Fugazi, bringing his emotional vocal style to one of post-hardcore's most important bands.
The emo revival saw a resurgence of house shows and DIY venues, harking back to the genre's underground punk roots.
Algernon Cadwallader, from Philadelphia, combined math rock intricacy with emo's emotional core, becoming a key emo revival influence.
My Chemical Romance's 'Three Cheers for Sweet Revenge' went triple platinum and featured the hit singles 'Helena' and 'I'm Not Okay (I Promise).'
The term 'skramz' was coined by screamo fans to differentiate real screamo from the watered-down mainstream usage of the term.
Fall Out Boy appeared on the cover of Rolling Stone in 2007, signaling emo's arrival as a mainstream cultural force.
Benton Falls, from Phoenix, Arizona, played an atmospheric brand of emo that drew comparisons to Sunny Day Real Estate and Mineral.
Guitar pedal boards in emo typically include delay, reverb, overdrive, and tremolo effects chained together for layered soundscapes.
The Starting Line's 'Say It Like You Mean It' (2002) was a staple of early 2000s emo-pop alongside The Academy Is... and Cartel.
Tapping harmonics—where guitarists lightly tap the string at harmonic nodes—create the bell-like tones heard in much Midwest emo.
Jimmy Eat World's 'Clarity' (1999) is often called the greatest emo album ever made, featuring the epic nine-minute closer 'Goodbye Sky Harbor.'
Conor Oberst of Bright Eyes released his first album at age 13, making him one of the youngest artists in the emo-adjacent scene.
Emo lyrics frequently reference specific locations, street names, and personal details, giving the genre an autobiographical documentary quality.
The Academy Is...'s William Beckett became a fashion icon in the emo scene, known for his slim-fitting suits and dramatic stage presence.
Coheed and Cambria blended post-hardcore and progressive rock into a sprawling science fiction concept spanning their entire discography.
The DIY screen-printed t-shirt and merch table was a crucial income source for underground emo bands touring in vans.
Owen, the solo project of American Football's Mike Kinsella, distills Midwest emo into intimate, acoustic-leaning songs.
Stretch Armstrong was an early melodic hardcore band on Equal Vision Records that influenced the emo-leaning side of hardcore punk.
Gerard Way cited David Bowie and Queen as major influences on My Chemical Romance's theatrical, concept-driven approach.
Fall Out Boy's 'Infinity on High' debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard 200, selling 260,000 copies in its first week.
The 'whoa-oh' gang vocal became a signature element of 2000s emo and pop-punk, appearing in countless choruses.
Cross My Heart, from Dallas, Texas, played a style of emo influenced by slowcore and dream pop.
Weezer's Rivers Cuomo has acknowledged the emotional vulnerability of 'Pinkerton' as something he initially regretted before it was embraced by emo fans.
Emo's influence on hip-hop became apparent in the late 2010s, with artists like Lil Peep and Juice WRLD blending emo melodies with trap beats.
Silverstein's Shane Told hosts the podcast 'Lead Singer Syndrome,' interviewing fellow musicians from the emo and post-hardcore world.
The Movielife, from Long Island, were part of the early 2000s post-hardcore scene and featured members who later joined Brand New and I Am the Avalanche.
Many emo bands recorded at Smart Studios in Madison, Wisconsin, where Butch Vig (producer of Nirvana's 'Nevermind') helmed sessions.
I Hate Myself, from Gainesville, Florida, were a sardonically named emo band whose overwrought emotionalism was simultaneously sincere and self-aware.
Panic! At The Disco evolved from baroque pop-emo to synth-pop over their career, with Brendon Urie becoming the sole constant member.
Brand New released their final album 'Science Fiction' in 2017, which debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard 200.
The Fender Twin Reverb amplifier's clean headroom and built-in reverb make it a popular amp choice for emo's shimmering guitar tones.
Cap'n Jazz only played together for about two years but spawned an entire family tree of influential emo and indie bands.
Acceptance's 'Phantoms' (2005) was a polished emo-rock album that gained a cult following despite the band breaking up shortly after its release.
Emo has always had a complicated relationship with the punk scene, with some purists rejecting it and others embracing it as punk's emotional evolution.
Hey Mercedes, featuring Bob Nanna of Braid, continued the Midwest emo tradition with a more polished, power-pop inflected sound.
Snowing, from Pennsylvania, were a short-lived emo revival band whose lo-fi recordings became hugely influential despite their brief existence.
Gerard Way revealed that 'The Black Parade' character was inspired by a dream about death appearing as a parade.
Boysetsfire blended post-hardcore with politically charged lyrics, bridging the gap between emo's personal focus and punk's activist tradition.
At the Drive-In broke up in 2001, with members forming The Mars Volta (progressive rock) and Sparta (post-hardcore).
The lyrical content of emo ranges from heartbreak and anxiety to existential dread and celebrations of friendship.
Armor for Sleep's concept album 'What to Do When You Are Dead' (2005) explored the afterlife through a post-hardcore lens.
The octave chord—playing the root and its octave simultaneously on guitar—is one of the most common techniques in emo rhythm guitar.
Mae's 'The Everglow' (2005) was a concept album that blended emo with electronic textures and cinematic compositions.
Emo's visual aesthetic was heavily influenced by punk zine culture, with cut-and-paste flyer designs remaining popular for show promotion.
Tim Kinsella of Cap'n Jazz and Joan of Arc has been called the 'godfather of Midwest emo' for his foundational role in the scene.
The number of vinyl reissues of classic emo albums exploded in the 2010s, with labels like Polyvinyl and Run for Cover meeting collector demand.
Dashboard Confessional's 'Screaming Infidelities' won the MTV2 Award at the 2002 VMAs, a landmark moment for emo on mainstream television.
Tiny Moving Parts combine emo with math rock technicality, featuring rapid-fire tapping riffs and energetic, upbeat delivery.
The 'emo gaze' subgenre fuses emo songwriting with shoegaze's wall-of-sound guitar textures, exemplified by bands like Nothing and Cloakroom.
Silverstein, from Burlington, Ontario, helped establish Canada as a significant contributor to the global post-hardcore scene.
Frank Iero of My Chemical Romance has pursued solo work as Frank Iero and the Patience (later ...and the Future Violents).
Early emo shows were often held in basements, VFW halls, and community centers, maintaining punk's tradition of all-ages, affordable events.
Fall Out Boy's album titles, like 'From Under the Cork Tree' (a reference to 'The Story of Ferdinand'), often draw from literature.
The Jealous Sound, featuring Blair Shehan formerly of Knapsack, was a critical darling of the early 2000s emo scene.
Empire! Empire! (I Was a Lonely Estate) were a Michigan emo revival band known for long song titles and delicate, intimate arrangements.
Rites of Spring performed just 15 shows in their brief career but radically changed what punk could sound like emotionally.
Adam Lazzara of Taking Back Sunday was famous for swinging his microphone by its cord during live performances.
Thursday's 'War All the Time' (2003) debuted at No. 7 on the Billboard 200, marking one of post-hardcore's biggest chart successes.
Bands like Turnover and Tigers Jaw straddled the line between emo revival and indie rock, expanding the genre's appeal.
The emo subculture maintained close ties with the straight edge movement, with many scene members abstaining from drugs and alcohol.
Moneen, from Brampton, Ontario, were a Canadian post-hardcore band known for their energetic live shows and experimental approach.
Hawthorne Heights faced a tragic loss when guitarist Casey Calvert passed away in 2007 while on tour with the band.
Merchant Ships, from Indianapolis, were an influential screamo and emo band who were active for just two years (2008–2010).
Emo's influence on fashion extended to nail polish on men, dyed black hair, and Chuck Taylor sneakers customized with marker drawings.
Further Seems Forever went through three vocalists, including Chris Carrabba (Dashboard Confessional) and Jason Gleason.
The 'twinkling' clean guitar tone in emo is often achieved by playing arpeggiated chords high on the neck while letting open strings ring out.
Knapsack, from San Francisco, bridged radio-friendly alternative rock with emo's emotional intensity in the late 1990s.
Rainer Maria, featuring vocalist and bassist Caithlin De Marrais, were one of the few prominent emo bands with a female lead.
Panic! At The Disco's music video for 'I Write Sins Not Tragedies' was set at a wedding and directed by Shane Drake.
Get Up Kids bassist Rob Pope later played in Spoon, bridging emo and indie rock worlds.
The 'noodly' guitar style of Midwest emo—all twisting melodies and unexpected chord changes—set it apart from more straightforward punk-rooted emo.
Basement, from Ipswich, England, brought UK post-hardcore and emo to a New Wave audience, earning a devoted American following.
Alesana combined screamo with literary concept albums, creating narratives inspired by Edgar Allan Poe and classic literature.
Underoath's 'Define the Great Line' debuted at No. 2 on the Billboard 200 in 2006, the highest chart position for a screamo-influenced act.
The dual-vocalist dynamic—one singing clean, one screaming—became a defining feature of 2000s post-hardcore.
American Football's iconic house in Urbana, Illinois, featured on their debut album cover, became a pilgrimage site for emo fans.
Mineral reunited in 2014 and released their third album, 'One Day When We Are Young,' in 2019 after a 22-year gap.
Emo kids frequently made mixtapes and burned CDs for friends, curating personal soundtracks of their favorite emotional songs.
The Swellers, from Flint, Michigan, blended emo with punk rock energy across four studio albums before breaking up in 2014.
Brand New's 'Deja Entendu' sold over 300,000 copies, launching the band from underground buzz to mainstream recognition.
Balance and Composure, from Doylestown, Pennsylvania, combined emo with grunge and alt-rock influences.
Emo drumming evolved from simple punk beats to complex patterns incorporating jazz influences and unconventional time signatures.
The influence of Dischord Records' 'Revolution Summer' of 1985 D.C. hardcore directly birthed what would become emo.
Joyce Manor's short, punchy songs rarely exceed two minutes, proving that emo can be concise as well as sprawling.
Saosin's 'Translating the Name' EP (2003), featuring Anthony Green on vocals, is considered a gold standard of post-hardcore.
The band name 'Fall Out Boy' comes from a character in 'The Simpsons'—Radioactive Man's sidekick.
Emery blended southern rock sensibility with post-hardcore aggression, gaining a strong following in both secular and Christian rock markets.
The emotional sincerity of emo lyrics stood in contrast to the ironic detachment of 1990s alternative rock.
Sorority Noise explored themes of mental health and loss with unflinching honesty, becoming a key voice in the emo revival.
Mewithoutyou combined post-hardcore with spoken word poetry and spiritual themes, drawing from Christian mysticism and Sufi poetry.
Jimmy Eat World recorded 'Bleed American' before it was released, investing $25,000 of their own money after being dropped by Capitol.
The 'screamo' label applies most accurately to bands like Saetia, Orchid, and pg.99 rather than mainstream acts like The Used or Senses Fail.
Taking Back Sunday have gone through numerous lineup changes, with Adam Lazzara remaining the sole constant member.
Emo photography favored dramatic black-and-white shots, extreme close-ups, and photos taken at odd angles.
The complexity of emo guitar work often requires musicians to use multiple guitars with different tunings during live sets.
Planes Mistaken for Stars played a chaotic, bourbon-soaked style of post-hardcore from Peoria, Illinois.
Elliott, named after Elliott Smith, were a Portland-based emo band whose dreamy sound predated the emo revival by over a decade.
Emo's second wave in the 1990s was centered around indie labels and college radio rather than mainstream media outlets.
Dashboard Confessional's 'A Mark, a Mission, a Brand, a Scar' debuted at No. 2 on the Billboard 200 in 2003.
Mike Kinsella has been a member of Cap'n Jazz, American Football, Owen, Their / They're / There, and numerous other projects, making him Midwest emo's most prolific figure.
Emo went through a period of critical reappraisal in the 2010s, with publications revisiting and reevaluating albums that were initially dismissed.
Copeland blended emo with atmospheric pop, featuring Aaron Marsh's delicate falsetto vocals.
Letters to Cleo's Kay Hanley cited early emo as an influence, showing the genre's quiet impact on adjacent alternative rock.
The 'emo court' meme—a fictional tribunal judging whether bands are 'real emo'—became one of the genre's most enduring online jokes.
Lifetime, from New Jersey, were an early '90s emo band whose reunion in 2006 was one of the scene's most celebrated comebacks.
Pianos Become the Teeth evolved from screamo to a subdued, atmospheric post-hardcore sound between their first and second albums.
The 'emo rap' movement of the late 2010s, featuring artists like XXXTentacion and Nothing,Nowhere., blended emo guitar samples with hip-hop production.
Title Fight transitioned from pop-punk to shoegaze-emo over their career, with 'Hyperview' (2015) being a fully shoegaze-influenced record.
From First to Last featured a young Sonny Moore (later known as Skrillex) as their vocalist on their first two albums.
Midtown, from New Brunswick, New Jersey, featured Gabe Saporta who later founded Cobra Starship.
The phrase 'you probably haven't heard of them' became an emo scene cliché about fans gatekeeping obscure bands.
Pedro the Lion's David Bazan struggled publicly with faith and doubt, themes that resonated across the emo community.
Real Friends, from Illinois, became one of the most streamed emo-pop bands of the mid-2010s.
Mineral's 'EndSerenading' (1998) is considered one of emo's most beautifully produced albums, with shimmering, layered guitars.
The compact disc booklet became a site for emo bands to include extensive lyrics, thank-you lists, and personal photographs.
Capn Jazz's 'Shmap'n Shmazz' compilation was where many fans first heard the band, as their original releases were hard to find.
Screamo shows in the 1990s and 2000s were notorious for their intensity, with bands playing on the floor at eye level with the audience.
The Story So Far, though more pop-punk than emo, drew heavily from emo's confessional lyrical tradition.
Emo's influence on mainstream pop became evident when artists like Olivia Rodrigo cited Paramore and emo bands as direct inspirations.
Rise Records signed Pierce the Veil, whose 'Collide with the Sky' (2012) debuted at No. 12 on the Billboard 200.
The term 'noodling' in emo guitar refers to complex, twisting melodic lines played over chord progressions.
Hardy Boys-style mystery novel covers and illustrated album art became a visual signature for many emo releases.
The Promise Ring dissolved partly due to frontman Davey von Bohlen suffering a brain aneurysm, though he recovered and continued making music.
Saves the Day's 'Stay What You Are' (2001) was a landmark emo album that balanced catchy melodies with anxious, yearning lyrics.
Grown-up emo fans, sometimes called 'elder emos,' have driven demand for nostalgia tours and anniversary album performances.
When We Were Young Festival in Las Vegas debuted in 2022 as a massive emo and pop-punk nostalgia festival.
Zolof the Rock & Roll Destroyer brought synth-heavy power pop to the emo scene with exuberant, upbeat energy.
The influence of Sonic Youth's dissonant guitar tunings can be heard throughout emo, from Sunny Day Real Estate to Brand New.
Moss Icon, from Annapolis, Maryland, were an early post-hardcore/emo band whose dynamic shifts from whisper to scream influenced all of screamo.
Andrew McMahon (Something Corporate, Jack's Mannequin) was diagnosed with leukemia in 2005 and documented his treatment, inspiring fans across the emo world.
Emo album cover art frequently featured melancholic imagery: lonely figures, empty rooms, suburban landscapes, and handwritten text.
Northstar, from Birmingham, Alabama, released two albums of shimmering emo-pop on Polyvinyl Record Co.
The use of trumpet and brass instruments appeared in bands like The World Is a Beautiful Place, adding post-rock grandeur to emo songs.
Nick Thomas of Spill Canvas created one of emo-pop's most quintessential love songs with 'All Over You.'
Lil Peep's 2017 album 'Come Over When You're Sober' brought emo melodies to a hip-hop audience, cementing the emo-rap crossover.
Emo has been called 'punk rock's emotional wing,' emphasizing vulnerability and personal expression over political anger.
Alex Gaskarth of All Time Low grew up attending emo shows in Baltimore and has cited Blink-182 and New Found Glory as key influences.
The Marshall JCM800 amplifier, cranked for natural overdrive, was a favored amp head in both post-hardcore and emo.
Emo's emphasis on authenticity meant that fans valued artists who appeared genuinely vulnerable over those who seemed to perform emotion.
The Weakerthans, while primarily indie rock, influenced the emo revival with John K. Samson's literary, emotionally precise songwriting.
Drive Like Jehu, from San Diego, were a proto-post-hardcore band featuring guitarist John Reis (also of Rocket from the Crypt).
Fall Out Boy's 'Thnks fr th Mmrs' used a deliberate vowel-dropping spelling style that became an emo cultural marker.
Mayday Parade formed in Tallahassee, Florida, and became one of the last bands to break through via the classic Myspace-to-Warped Tour pipeline.
The Appleseed Cast's 'Mare Vitalis' (2000) pushed emo into post-rock territory with its expansive, instrumental-heavy approach.
Emo fan communities migrated from Myspace to Tumblr in the late 2000s, where GIF culture and band memes flourished.
Chris Simpson of Mineral went on to join The Gloria Record, continuing his band's atmospheric emo legacy.
The split between Drive-Thru Records bands (pop-punk) and Vagrant Records bands (emo) illustrated the genre's internal diversity.
Emo shows often featured stage diving, crowd surfing, and audience sing-alongs that created a communal, cathartic atmosphere.
Underoath broke up in 2013 but reunited in 2015, subsequently releasing the acclaimed album 'Erase Me.'
Emo bands influenced by Weezer often featured power-pop chord progressions dressed up with distortion and confessional lyrics.
The independent label Count Your Lucky Stars Records became a hub for 2010s emo revival releases.
Sunny Day Real Estate's William Goldsmith went on to drum for Foo Fighters before returning to SDRE for their second reunion.
Post-hardcore pioneer Refused's 'The Shape of Punk to Come' (1998) anticipated many of the genre's future innovations.
The emo revival saw bands like Dikembe and Dowsing champion a more laid-back, slacker-influenced approach to the genre.
Emo guitarists frequently use tremolo picking—rapidly alternating pick strokes on a single note—to create intensity in climactic passages.
The band name 'My Chemical Romance' was suggested by bassist Mikey Way, inspired by Irvine Welsh's novel 'Ecstasy: Three Tales of Chemical Romance.'
Anberlin, from Orlando, Florida, blended emo and post-hardcore with anthemic, stadium-ready choruses.
Emo's definition has been debated more fiercely than almost any other genre, with fans, critics, and musicians all offering contradictory takes.
The 'emo' episode of South Park in 2012 satirized the subculture, generating both backlash and amusement within the community.
Senses Fail frontman Buddy Nielsen has been open about his struggles with addiction and mental health, using music as a form of therapy.
Run for Cover Records, based in Boston, became a leading label for modern emo and indie rock in the 2010s.
Emo's lyrical intimacy—addressing specific people, places, and incidents—gives the genre a confessional, almost diaristic quality.
At the Drive-In reunited in 2012 and released 'in•ter a•li•a' in 2017, their first new album in 17 years.
Emo songwriting often employs the quiet-loud-quiet dynamic borrowed from post-rock and grunge, building from whispered verses to cathartic screamed choruses.
Plain White T's 'Hey There Delilah,' though pop-rock, emerged from the Midwest emo scene and reflects its emotional sincerity.
The guitar riff from Jimmy Eat World's 'Sweetness' is one of the most instantly recognizable in emo history.
Desaparecidos, Conor Oberst's punk side project, brought political urgency to a scene often criticized for being too inward-looking.
Emo's influence extended to anime and manga fan communities, where the visual aesthetics and emotional intensity found natural overlap.
Paramore's Hayley Williams became one of the most prominent female figures in a genre that was often criticized for being male-dominated.
Topshelf Records, co-founded in Peabody, Massachusetts, released albums by The World Is a Beautiful Place and Pianos Become the Teeth.
Chris Carrabba's songwriting for Dashboard Confessional often features second-person address, drawing listeners directly into the narrative.
The compilation 'Emo Diaries' series on Deep Elm Records ran for 12 volumes, documenting the genre across more than a decade.
Emo gained a foothold in the UK through bands like Funeral for a Friend, Hundred Reasons, and Hell Is for Heroes.
Gerard Way earned a BFA from the School of Visual Arts in New York City before starting My Chemical Romance.
Emo's third wave coincided with the rise of social media, with Myspace, PureVolume, and AbsolutePunk.net serving as key tastemakers.
Jets to Brazil, formed by Blake Schwarzenbach after Jawbreaker, pursued a more polished, indie-rock-oriented evolution of emo.
Fall Out Boy's Patrick Stump released a solo R&B-influenced album, 'Soul Punk,' in 2011 that was critically praised but commercially modest.
The phrase 'rawr XD' became an iconic internet expression associated with emo and scene culture in the mid-2000s.
Dance Gavin Dance have maintained a prolific output, releasing over ten studio albums and cycling through multiple vocalists.
The 'emo mullet'—short in front, long in back—was a hairstyle variation that appeared in the more hardcore-influenced corners of the scene.
AFI's 'Sing the Sorrow' (2003) marked the band's transition from hardcore punk to a gothic, emo-influenced sound.
Equal Vision Records released albums by Saves the Day, Coheed and Cambria, and many other emo-adjacent acts.
Emo fans are known for their deep loyalty, following bands through multiple breakups, reunions, and reinventions.
John Nolan left Taking Back Sunday to form Straylight Run, later returning to TBS for their reunion.
The 'American Football house' at 704 W. High Street in Urbana, Illinois, has been photographed by thousands of emo fans on pilgrimage.
Emo's emphasis on community over stardom mirrored punk's anti-celebrity ethos, with band members often mixing with fans at shows.
Tigers Jaw's 'Charmer' (2014) was a tender, melodic album that helped establish the Scranton, Pennsylvania, band as emo revival favorites.
The Receiving End of Sirens blended post-hardcore with progressive and jazz elements, pushing genre boundaries with their 2005 debut 'Between the Heart and the Synapse.'
Emo bands in the 2020s increasingly incorporate elements of bedroom pop, lo-fi, and R&B into their sound.
The Dangerous Summer, from Levittown, Pennsylvania, brought an atmospheric, anthemic quality to 2010s emo-rock.
Maximum the Hormone, a Japanese band, blended post-hardcore with pop, metal, and absurdist humor, showing emo's global variation.
The Promise Ring's 'Very Emergency' (1999) pivoted toward new wave and power pop, dividing fans of their earlier emo sound.
Emo's emphasis on personal growth narratives—songs about overcoming heartbreak, finding identity—gives the genre an aspirational quality alongside its sadness.
Bob Nanna of Braid has been a quietly influential figure in Midwest emo for over 25 years.
Ray Toro of My Chemical Romance is celebrated for his guitar solos, particularly the iconic solo in 'Welcome to the Black Parade.'
Saosin's self-titled debut album (2006) debuted at No. 17 on the Billboard 200 under vocalist Cove Reber.
Emo's DIY recording tradition means many classic albums were recorded in basements, bedrooms, and makeshift studios.
The emo revival helped normalize conversations about mental health in music, with artists speaking openly about anxiety and depression.
Crash of Rhinos, from Nottingham, England, brought Midwest emo-influenced guitar work to the UK underground scene.
Bandcamp became the primary distribution platform for independent emo releases in the 2010s, replacing Myspace.
Brendan Lukens of Modern Baseball has been candid about their mental health struggles, contributing to emo's ongoing dialogue about vulnerability.
The Fender Telecaster, while less common than offset guitars in emo, is favored by some players for its bright, cutting tone.
You Blew It!, from Orlando, Florida, combined emo with power pop and intricate guitar work.
Free Throw, from Nashville, Tennessee, blend Midwest emo with indie rock and subtle Southern influences.
The band Dogleg's debut 'Melee' (2020) was celebrated as one of the most energetic emo-adjacent albums of the decade.
Emo and post-hardcore vocalists often sang about specific people using their real names, a level of lyrical directness unusual in rock music.
The twin-guitar attack—two guitarists playing complementary, interlocking parts—became a sonic hallmark of Midwest emo.
Yellowcard's use of a violin, played by Sean Mackin, gave their emo-pop sound a distinctive orchestral quality.
Bayside, from Queens, New York, maintained a devoted following through consistent touring and reliably strong albums.
Embrace's self-titled album (1987) was one of Ian MacKaye's most personal works, predating many of the themes emo would later explore.
The phrase 'the scene' was used by emo and post-hardcore fans to describe their interconnected community of bands, fans, and venues.
Anthony Green of Circa Survive is known for his improvisational live vocal performances, rarely singing a song the same way twice.
The use of delay and reverb effects to create cascading, ambient guitar swells is sometimes called 'the emo wash.'
Emo's relationship with irony is complex—songs often walk a tightrope between genuine emotion and self-aware melodrama.
Jimmy Eat World's 'Futures' (2004) continued the band's emo-rock trajectory, featuring the hit 'Pain.'
Heart attack Man, from Cleveland, combine emo with grunge and noise rock for a heavier take on the genre.
